sections.text: Welcome back. AP Psychology, Module Three — biological bases of behavior. Today we crack open the brain. By the end you'll know how a single neuron fires, how brain regions divide up the work, and how chemicals you've never thought about are quietly steering your mood right now. This is one of the heaviest content modules in the course. Let's go.

Quick scenario. It's two a.m. You promised yourself you'd sleep at midnight. But you're still scrolling. Why? Honestly — because your brain is doing chemistry. Every funny video, every notification, every like — that's a tiny hit of dopamine, a neurotransmitter that says 'do that again.' Meanwhile caffeine you drank at four p.m. is still blocking the receptor that's supposed to make you sleepy. You are not weak-willed. You are a biological machine being hijacked by molecules. The whole module today is about those molecules, the cells they live in, and the brain regions running the show. Your brain is doing this RIGHT NOW.

Four things today. One — the neuron, the basic unit. Two — how neurons talk to each other and the key neurotransmitters AP wants you to know. Three — the major brain regions, from the brainstem up to the cortex. Four — the endocrine system and the nature-versus-nurture debate. A lot of vocabulary today. No shortcut here. Memorize.

The neuron. Roughly eighty-six billion of them in your skull. Each one has the same basic shape. Dendrites — the branchy bits that receive signals from other neurons. The soma — the cell body, the headquarters. The axon — a long cable that carries the signal away. Wrapped around the axon, in patches, is the myelin sheath — fatty insulation that makes the signal travel way faster. At the end, terminal buttons — little knobs that pass the signal on to the next neuron. Dendrites in. Soma processes. Axon out. Memorize that flow.

How does the signal actually travel down the axon? The action potential. Here's the AP-level intuition. A neuron at rest is slightly negative inside compared to outside. When enough input arrives at the dendrites, the neuron hits a threshold, and BAM — sodium ions rush in, the inside flips positive, and that flip races down the axon like a wave. Then it resets. The key word — all-or-nothing. The neuron either fires fully or it doesn't fire at all. There is no 'half-fire.' A stronger stimulus doesn't make a bigger spike — it makes the neuron spike more often. AP loves this distinction. All-or-nothing. Frequency, not size.

When the action potential reaches the terminal buttons, it has to jump to the next neuron — but there's a tiny gap called the synapse. Electrical signals can't jump that gap, so the neuron switches to chemistry. The terminal buttons release neurotransmitters — chemical messengers — into the gap. Those molecules float across, and bind to receptors on the next neuron's dendrites like keys into locks. If enough keys turn, the next neuron fires. Then the leftover neurotransmitters are either broken down or pulled back into the original neuron — that's reuptake. Most antidepressants work by blocking reuptake, leaving more neurotransmitter in the gap. Just so you know what 'SSRI' actually means.

Six neurotransmitters AP wants you to know. Dopamine — reward, motivation, and movement. Too little and you can get Parkinson's. Too much in certain pathways is linked to schizophrenia. Serotonin — mood, sleep, appetite. Low serotonin is linked to depression. Glutamate — the main excitatory neurotransmitter. It says GO. GABA — the main inhibitory one. It says STOP. Anti-anxiety meds boost GABA. Acetylcholine — memory and muscle movement. Alzheimer's involves losing acetylcholine neurons. And endorphins — your body's natural painkillers, released during exercise or injury. That's runner's high. Six names. Memorize what each one does.

Your turn. A new medication makes people feel calmer and less anxious by boosting one specific neurotransmitter. Based on what we just covered, which neurotransmitter is the drug most likely targeting, and is it excitatory or inhibitory? Pause. Decide. Press play.

Welcome back. The neurotransmitter is GABA — gamma-aminobutyric acid — and it is inhibitory. Anti-anxiety medications like benzodiazepines boost GABA, which calms neurons down across the brain. If you said serotonin, fair guess — SSRIs do treat anxiety too — but serotonin's main lane on the AP exam is mood. The 'calmer, less anxious' wording with 'inhibitory' as the second clue points straight at GABA. Glutamate is the excitatory opposite. Memorize the GABA-glutamate pair. They're the brain's gas and brake pedals.

Now zoom out to the whole brain. Three big divisions. Hindbrain — the oldest part, sitting on top of your spinal cord. The medulla controls heartbeat and breathing. The pons helps with sleep and coordination. The cerebellum, that little cauliflower at the back, runs balance and fine motor skills. Midbrain — small, mostly handles arousal and reflex orientation. Forebrain — the big one. The thalamus is the brain's switchboard, relaying sensory info to the right place. The hypothalamus runs homeostasis — hunger, thirst, body temperature. The hippocampus forms new memories — damage it and you can't make new ones. The amygdala handles fear and emotional intensity. Together the hippocampus, amygdala, hypothalamus and thalamus are the limbic system — emotion central.

On top of all that — the cerebral cortex. The wrinkled outer layer where the human stuff happens. Four lobes per side. The frontal lobe, behind your forehead, handles planning, decision-making, and personality. It also contains Broca's area, which produces speech. The parietal lobe, top of the head, handles touch and body position. The temporal lobe, by the ears, handles hearing — and contains Wernicke's area, which understands language. The occipital lobe, all the way at the back, handles vision. Memorize this map. Frontal — plan. Parietal — touch. Temporal — hear. Occipital — see. AP will absolutely give you a lobe-damage scenario and ask what gets disrupted.

Two more systems and we're done. The endocrine system — glands that release hormones into your bloodstream. Slower than neurons but longer-lasting. The pituitary is the master gland, sitting under the hypothalamus, telling the other glands what to do. The adrenal glands, on top of your kidneys, dump adrenaline when you're scared — that's fight-or-flight, that pre-presentation jolt. The thyroid controls metabolism. And then — genetics. Twin studies are AP gold. Identical twins share a hundred percent of DNA, fraternal twins share fifty. If a trait is more similar in identical twins, genes matter. But heritability is not destiny — even highly heritable traits get shaped by environment. Nature loads the gun. Nurture pulls the trigger. That's the modern view.

Recap. Neuron parts — dendrites, soma, axon, myelin, terminals. Action potentials are all-or-nothing — strength is coded in frequency. Synapse equals neurotransmitter crossing a gap, binding, then reuptake. The big six — dopamine for reward, serotonin for mood, glutamate excites, GABA inhibits, acetylcholine for memory and movement, endorphins for pain. Hindbrain runs survival, midbrain is small, forebrain is the limbic system plus thalamus. Cortex lobes — frontal plans, parietal feels, temporal hears, occipital sees. Endocrine system uses hormones — pituitary is master, adrenals do fight-or-flight. Twin studies show heritability, but environment still shapes the outcome.

Important — this video is the overview. To actually master Module Three, here's the path. One — read Myers Psychology for AP, Module Three, the brain and neural communication chapters. Read it twice. Two — go to AP Classroom and try fifteen multiple-choice questions on neuron parts, neurotransmitters, and brain regions. Three — do the assignment in your dashboard: match ten brain-damage scenarios to the affected region. Four — book a check-in with your advisor if action potentials or limbic system anatomy still feels fuzzy — this module trips most students up. Five — make a brain map. Draw the brain. Label every region we covered. Print it. Tape it where you'll see it daily. See you in Module Four — sensation and perception, where we figure out how all this hardware turns light, sound, and pressure into actual experience.
